title: Dont use symlink in docker archive format
body:
This might not be a bug as much as a request. But when converting to a dock-archive file symlinks are used for the layer tar. I use skopeo for converting different docker image format to docker archive format so i can scan them to find the SBOM and the symlinks are causing the scanner to find bad results. 

For example if i run `skopeo copy docker://hello-world:latest  docker-archive:/tmp/test/hello-world.tar` I get the following file structure:
```
a803ca04f00ecd1c10fb4978f7a963d1461f4225e568901bc6990cb46a3e04be/layer.tar
a803ca04f00ecd1c10fb4978f7a963d1461f4225e568901bc6990cb46a3e04be/json
a803ca04f00ecd1c10fb4978f7a963d1461f4225e568901bc6990cb46a3e04be/VERSION
repositories
manifest.json
d1165f2212346b2bab48cb01c1e39ee8ad1be46b87873d9ca7a4e434980a7726.json
f22b99068db93900abe17f7f5e09ec775c2826ecfe9db961fea68293744144bd.tar
```
For this `a803ca04f00ecd1c10fb4978f7a963d1461f4225e568901bc6990cb46a3e04be/layer.tar` is a symlink to `f22b99068db93900abe17f7f5e09ec775c2826ecfe9db961fea68293744144bd.tar`. 

It would be helpful if symlinks weren't used and the docker-archive was the same format as doing `docker save hello-world:latest > hello-world.tar` which results in something like 
```
a803ca04f00ecd1c10fb4978f7a963d1461f4225e568901bc6990cb46a3e04be/layer.tar
a803ca04f00ecd1c10fb4978f7a963d1461f4225e568901bc6990cb46a3e04be/json
a803ca04f00ecd1c10fb4978f7a963d1461f4225e568901bc6990cb46a3e04be/VERSION
repositories
manifest.json
d1165f2212346b2bab48cb01c1e39ee8ad1be46b87873d9ca7a4e434980a7726.json
```

comments.body: A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

Thanks for your report.

Most importantly, if you don’t _need_ `docker-archive:` to interoperate, try hard to avoid it. It’s a limited format (e.g. can’t represent OCI images natively) and it’s almost always unnecessarily costly to use (almost all uses will involve creating a possibly multi-gigabyte single file, or maybe several copies of it). Almost always one of `docker:` or `dir:` is more efficient; for a code dealing with details of images, maybe even a specialized `containers/image/types.ImageTransport` implementation might be worth it.

Symbolic links are necessary to generate “legacy-format”-compatible archives that contain multiple references of the same layer (whether within a single image, or commonly with multiple images that share a base image), without duplicating the potentially huge layers. Docker also uses them in some cases, see https://github.com/moby/moby/blob/c81abefdb1f907bbc5f5b8b1b1fba942821ae5b3/image/tarexport/save.go#L384 . So any consumer of `docker-archive:`-formatted files, even if the file is only expected to contain a single image, must handle symbolic links already — and then I don’t think it makes much difference of _how exactly_ the symbolic links are used; the consumer should read `manifest.json` to find the right files instead of presuming any specific file structure.
